Title: Beet red food colourant can be produced more sustainably with engineered Yarrowia lipolytica

Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Efficiently stabilizes betanidin into betanin and increases betacyanin production relative to DbB5GT.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. BvSGT2 completed the heterologous pathway by glycosylating betanidin to betanin. 2. In Y. lipolytica it produced ~9.9 mg l^-1 betacyanins (8.6 mg l^-1 betanin, 1.3 mg l^-1 isobetanin), nearly threefold more than DbB5GT. 3. Betanin was distributed in both cells (~40%) and supernatant (~60%).
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Combinatorial pathway expression; HPLC quantification of betanin/isobetanin; LC–MS support for pathway intermediates
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: heterologously expressed in engineered Y. lipolytica
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful heterologous glycosyltransferase for microbial betanin production because it improves stable betanin formation.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Chosen over DbB5GT because of much better betacyanin output.

Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Supported betanin formation but was less efficient than BvSGT2.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. DbB5GT was tested as an alternative betanidin glycosyltransferase. 2. It yielded substantially lower betacyanin production than BvSGT2. 3. The comparison identified BvSGT2 as the superior enzyme for pathway completion in Y. lipolytica.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Comparative heterologous expression and HPLC quantification
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Serves as a benchmark glycosyltransferase but is not preferred for engineering.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Used for direct enzyme comparison only.

Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes betanin deglycosylation/degradation; deletion helped retain betanin during fermentation.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. A degradation assay of 14 putative beta-glucosidases identified YALI1_B18845g as highly involved in betanin deglycosylation. 2. Disrupting this gene together with YALI1_B18887g in the production strain improved betanin retention. 3. The double-deletion strain reached 1,271 mg l^-1 betanin and retained 3.6-fold more betanin at 66 h.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Targeted gene disruptions; betanin degradation assay; fed-batch fermentation profiling
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: native gene in Y. lipolytica production strain
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Deletion target for preventing product loss in microbial betanin cell factories.
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Fed-batch fermentation; degradation observed after peak production
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Acts negatively on product stability rather than biosynthesis.

Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes betanin deglycosylation/degradation; deletion helped retain betanin during fermentation.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. YALI1_B18887g was identified among 14 candidate beta-glucosidases as highly involved in betanin deglycosylation. 2. Joint disruption with YALI1_B18845g increased betanin retention during fed-batch fermentation. 3. The resulting strain achieved record betacyanin titre and markedly improved product stability after peak production.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Targeted gene disruptions; betanin degradation assay; fed-batch fermentation profiling
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: native gene in Y. lipolytica production strain
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful deletion target to reduce downstream product degradation in industrial strains.
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Fed-batch fermentation; degradation observed after peak production
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Acts negatively on product stability rather than biosynthesis.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Hydroxylates L-tyrosine to L-DOPA and also oxidizes L-DOPA toward dopaquinone/cyclo-DOPA formation.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: supplies essential precursors for betanin formation
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Supported as a key control point by variant screening and copy-number optimization
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes betanin biosynthesis and was the preferred TYH for pathway assembly.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Among TYH variants tested, EvTYH with MjDOD gave the strongest betaxanthin/betanidin production, outperforming other combinations by more than twofold. 2. EvTYH was retained in the optimized production strains. 3. Increasing pathway copy number further increased betacyanin titres, indicating flux control at heterologous pathway enzymes including EvTYH.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Combinatorial enzyme screening; fluorescence screening; LC–MS identification of pathway products; HPLC quantification; pathway copy-number increase
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: heterologously expressed in engineered Y. lipolytica
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Mineral medium cultivation; fed-batch fermentation after pathway optimization
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Core heterologous enzyme choice for constructing high-flux microbial betanin production strains.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Can contribute to side chemistry and unstable intermediates if downstream glycosylation/conditions are suboptimal.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Alternative TYHs from Beta vulgaris and Abronia nealleyi were inferior in this host.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Cleaves L-DOPA to 4,5-seco-DOPA, which spontaneously cyclizes to betalamic acid.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: forms the chromophore precursor betalamic acid required for betanin assembly
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Supported as a key control point by variant screening and copy-number optimization
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Best-performing DOD variant for Y. lipolytica betanin production when paired with EvTYH.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. MjDOD combined with EvTYH gave the highest initial pathway performance among tested DOD/TYH combinations. 2. It supported betanidin formation verified by LC–MS. 3. Additional pathway copies increased final betacyanin titre, indicating this heterologous enzyme contributed to overall pathway flux limitation.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Combinatorial enzyme screening; fluorescence screening; LC–MS identification of intermediates; HPLC quantification; pathway copy-number increase
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: heterologously expressed in engineered Y. lipolytica
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Mineral medium cultivation; fed-batch fermentation after pathway optimization
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Essential heterologous enzyme for efficient microbial formation of betalamic acid and betacyanins.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: BgDOD2 was tested but performed less well in Y. lipolytica.

Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: shikimate pathway / aromatic amino acid biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes the first committed step of the shikimate pathway to increase aromatic amino acid precursor formation.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: supplies L-tyrosine precursor pool for betanin biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Engineered as key precursor-supply control point via feedback-insensitive allele
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Feedback-insensitive YlARO4 improved precursor supply and raised betacyanin production when added to the heterologous pathway.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Introducing YlARO4K221L with YlARO7G141S shifted pathway output toward betanidin and away from anthranilate–betaxanthin signatures. 2. In a betanin-producing strain, the pair improved betacyanin production 2.1-fold to 21.7 mg l^-1. 3. The result established aromatic precursor supply as a major engineering target.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Overexpression of feedback-insensitive allele; HPLC quantification; UV–vis spectral shift; LC–MS confirmation
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful precursor-pathway engineering lever for boosting tyrosine-derived products in Y. lipolytica.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: May increase upstream aromatic flux broadly, potentially affecting other aromatic metabolites.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Paper text reports K221L as feedback-insensitive allele.

Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: shikimate pathway / aromatic amino acid biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Converts chorismate to prephenate, directing carbon flux toward phenylalanine/tyrosine biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: increases flux toward the L-tyrosine pool feeding betanin biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Engineered as key precursor-supply control point via feedback-insensitive allele
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: In combination with YlARO4K221L increased betacyanin production and improved productive precursor routing.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. The feedback-insensitive YlARO7G141S was coexpressed with YlARO4K221L to enhance aromatic amino acid biosynthesis. 2. The combination raised betacyanin titres 2.1-fold in the BvSGT2-containing production strain. 3. Results demonstrated precursor limitation upstream of the heterologous betanin pathway.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Overexpression of feedback-insensitive allele; HPLC quantification; UV–vis spectral shift; LC–MS confirmation
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Important for precursor pathway reinforcement in tyrosine-derived product engineering.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: May alter broader aromatic amino acid flux.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Mutation is described as feedback-insensitive and paired with YlARO4 engineering.

Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: L-tyrosine catabolism / homogentisic acid side pathway
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Converts 4-hydroxyphenylpyruvate to homogentisic acid in tyrosine catabolism, promoting pyomelanin formation.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: competes for tyrosine-derived flux and diverts carbon away from betanin
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key competing step identified by deletion analysis
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Deletion eliminated major side-flux and increased betacyanin production 2.1-fold.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Yl4HPPD was identified as the tyrosine-catabolic step leading to homogentisic acid and pyomelanin. 2. Disrupting Yl4HPPD made colonies redder and increased betacyanin production to 118.1 mg l^-1 betanin plus 17.4 mg l^-1 isobetanin. 3. The improvement exceeded the gain from adding another heterologous pathway copy, indicating strong flux diversion to HGA before deletion.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Targeted gene disruption; shake-flask comparison; HPLC quantification; phenotypic observation of browning/redness
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: native gene disrupted in engineered Y. lipolytica
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Buffered media at pH ~5–6 accentuated browning and side-product formation
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Prime deletion target to block tyrosine catabolic loss and improve titre in microbial betanin production.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Could perturb native tyrosine catabolism and related physiology, though specific penalties were not reported.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Browning persisted after deletion, suggesting additional eumelanin-like chemistry independent of pyomelanin.
